abstract: This tart and often charming new comedy is a version of the heart-of-darkness teen social comedy "Heathers" for the tweener audience; it has the ambitious and cartoony dark side of a Disney Channel sitcom, with a lively and talented young cast headed by the cherubic Lindsay Lohan. The "Saturday Night Live" writer and performer Tina Fey wrote the script and gives the material a stylized vitality. But it does add a be-yourself finale that, while standard for such movies, this picture doesn't quite seem to buy — it's the kind of bored manipulation you come to expect from the Plastics, the pack of hallway-shrewd and glamorous teen queens who rule, through subtle terror, the high school that Cady (Ms. Lohan) attends. It's her first brush with socialization, since Cady was home schooled by her zoologist parents, who raised their daughter while traveling in Africa. Since most of "Girls" consists of the outsider Cady observing the tribal rites of her new setting and laying it all out in narration, this movie is great home for the meticulous and ruthless deadpan that Ms. Fey has perfected for the satirical "S.N.L." newscast. Though narrative cohesion isn't the strength of "Girls" — as a series of moments, it works better from scene to scene than as a whole — the intelligence shines in its understanding of contradictions. Scene by scene, you can't help but be impressed by "Girls"; it's like a group of sketches linked by theme, with some playing much better than others. But after a while, it does begin to seem like it has the easily diverted attention span of one of its archetypal high school kids — you feel a little like a scold for wanting it to buckle down and concentrate. — Elvis Mitchell

abstract: WHOO, HOT: Tatum O'Neal's memoir, ''A Paper Life,'' makes its debut on the nonfiction list this week at No. 4. Booksellers aren't surprised -- O'Neal pulled off a rare P.R. trifecta: she sat down with Oprah and Katie Couric, and she snagged part of a People magazine cover. O'Neal's memoir scorches a lot of earth. (There's that opium-and-orgy scene with Melanie Griffith and Maria Schneider when the author was barely in her teens, and . . . oh, never mind.) When it comes time for the paperback edition, O'Neal may have her hands on one of the great blurbs of all time. It comes from Oprah herself, who began her program with O'Neal by intoning: ''I mean, whoo, what a book. Ooh, I was reading it this weekend. Hot, hot, whoo, hot. In it -- oh, man. I mean, it's one of those books where you just say, 'I'm just going to tell everything.' ''    MEAN GIRLS: The success of O'Neal's tell-all is another indication that, in terms of The Times's nonfiction list, it's been a good season for the kind of brassy dames who put their pictures on the front, not the back, of their books. Memoirs by Paris Hilton and the porn star Jenna Jameson have had solid runs. Amy Fisher, the Long Island Lolita, has a new memoir, published only in paperback, called ''If I Knew Then. . . .'' (Fisher is at No. 34 on the extended nonfiction paperback list.) And Ann Coulter wears leather on the cover of ''How to Talk to a Liberal (if You Must),'' which is reviewed in this issue. Her book is at No. 2 in its second week.
